queued_publication_packets.title: Does every 5-regular pseudograph contain a {4,1}-factor?
queued_publication_packets.publication_if_solved: A full resolution of the smallest unresolved Akbari-Kano-type odd-degree case would already read like a short standalone paper.
queued_publication_packets.why_this_could_be_publishable: It is a named-conjecture residue with a clean yes-or-no endpoint, tiny verifier, and immediate title theorem if solved.
queued_publication_packets.canonical_source: Maria Axenovich and Jonathan Rollin, "Brooks type results for conflict-free colorings and {a,b}-factors in graphs" (Discrete Mathematics 338, 2015), plus Douglas West's regular-subgraph problem summary.

queued_publication_packets.title: Are all graphs on 9 vertices 1-11-representable?
queued_publication_packets.publication_if_solved: Settling the 9-vertex slice would already be a finite-classification note, either as a complete positive census or as a first minimal counterexample paper.
queued_publication_packets.why_this_could_be_publishable: It is exactly the kind of smallest unresolved finite slice that can become a clean note or a minimal-counterexample paper with low novelty-check cost.
queued_publication_packets.canonical_source: Mohammed Alshammari, Sergey Kitaev, Chaoliang Tang, Tianyi Tao, and Junchi Zhang, "On 1-11-representability and multi-1-11-representability of graphs" (Utilitas Mathematica 122, 2025), together with Futorny-Kitaev-Pyatkin, "New Tools to Study 1-11-Representation of Graphs" (Graphs and Combinatorics 40, 2024).

queued_publication_packets.title: Does every Paley graph have an internal partition?
queued_publication_packets.publication_if_solved: A complete Paley-family theorem is immediately paper-ready because the family is classical and the statement is already singled out as a named open problem.
queued_publication_packets.why_this_could_be_publishable: It is already phrased as a standalone open theorem on a famous graph family, so one strong solve is essentially the paper.
queued_publication_packets.canonical_source: Pál Bärnkopf, Zoltán Lóránt Nagy, and Zoltán Paulovics, "A Note on Internal Partitions: The 5-Regular Case and Beyond" (Graphs and Combinatorics, 2024), Problem 7.

queued_publication_packets.title: Does every 4-regular pseudograph contain a 3-regular subgraph?
queued_publication_packets.publication_if_solved: The pseudograph extension of a classical 4-regular theorem is already paper-shaped as a standalone yes/no result.
queued_publication_packets.why_this_could_be_publishable: It is an exact extension problem with a clean theorem statement and a short route from proof to paper headline once the statement boundary is refreshed.
queued_publication_packets.canonical_source: Fields Institute talk page "(3,1)-colorings of 4-regular graphs" (2016), summarizing Bernshteyn's 2014 reformulation and the remaining ordered-(3,1)-coloring gap.

queued_publication_packets.title: Does a conference graph of order 85 exist?
queued_publication_packets.publication_if_solved: An exact existence or nonexistence result for the smallest open conference case is automatically a paper.
queued_publication_packets.why_this_could_be_publishable: It is a canonical exact existence question with essentially zero post-solve packaging overhead.
queued_publication_packets.canonical_source: Makhnev et al., "Strongly regular graphs with non-trivial automorphisms" (Discrete Mathematics 311, 2011), cross-checked against recent conference-graph reference summaries.
